{"text": "Finding the course in the portal was straightforward. The search and filters got me to the Master of Business quickly, and it was clear what I was selecting (including that MBA sits separately). I didn\u2019t feel stuck or confused at that step. I used the search and filters organically, I hope it is noted I did not use the tab functions because I am a big user of the search functions."}
{"value": 4}

{"text": "Overall the form flow was solid — it’s structured properly and it mostly lets you move fast without thinking too hard. I could immediately see the main sections/tabs (personal info, program details, previous study), so I knew what the “journey” looked like before I started. 

What worked well:
 - Speed + efficiency: autofill helped, and I naturally went straight for mandatory fields and kept moving. That’s a good sign — the form supports the way real people complete applications (quick scan, fill, advance). 
 - It didn’t feel cluttered: the steps were clear enough that I wasn’t guessing where to go next. 

Where it fell down (and why it matters):
 - Work experience / entry requirements created friction and anxiety. I hit the work experience part and immediately thought: “I need to check the actual entry requirements right now so I know what evidence to upload and how to present myself.” I didn’t want to hunt around in a different tab or risk losing my place — I wanted a simple in-form link that says “Check requirements for program code #### here” and takes me to the exact requirement list. Without that, I got a bit nervous and it interrupts momentum. This is important because applicants are trying to be competitive — they want the “right” proof ready to go, not guesswork. 
 - The “saved” status wasn’t obvious enough. I only properly noticed the saved message late in the process. That should be much more prominent — ideally centred or persistent — with wording like “Saved — you can come back anytime.” People need that reassurance or they’ll abandon the form. 
 - Indigenous identification wasn’t handled well enough. Two issues here:
1. it needs a clear “prefer not to say” option for self-identification data, and it should be more front-end in the journey (registration or early in the application), including the ability to de-identify. 
2. it needs a plain-English “why we ask” that links to support — e.g., “We ask because UQ has services available; would you like the Aboriginal unit to contact you?” Without that, it risks feeling like you’re just being counted for reporting/funding. 
 - Clickable info links felt risky. When a form says “check your program details” I hesitated because I didn’t want it to break the flow or kick me out of the form. Those links should open clearly in a new tab with a “return to application” breadcrumb so users don’t fear losing progress. 
 - Submission reassurance: at the end, I was looking for a clear confirmation (ideally email confirmation) so I know it’s actually lodged."}

The layout is easy to scan, the imagery felt genuinely diverse, and key actions like contact us, how to apply and get ready are visible without me digging. The site search also worked well and got me where I needed to go quickly. \n\nNegatives: the Aboriginal support offer isn\u2019t front-and-centre in the student journey. I could see the Acknowledgement and reconciliation content, but as a prospective student I want a clearer, more obvious pathway to Aboriginal unit supports (and why/when you\u2019d use them), not something you have to hunt for."}

{"text": "The best thing was that the portal behaved the way you’d expect a serious enrolment system to behave, it let me move through the process quickly, without getting lost, and it had a few “guardrails” that stop dumb mistakes.

What stood out most:
 - It was easy to get back to the application and keep moving. I could return to the application form without hunting around, which matters because people bounce in and out (double-checking details, uploading docs, etc.). 
 - The document upload experience was genuinely smooth. Drag-and-drop worked straight away, and it didn’t feel fragile or fiddly. 
 - Good safeguards: when I tried uploading the same document again, it flagged it — that’s exactly the kind of simple protection users need. 
 - Clear enough progress cues: I could see it was saving, and I wasn’t left guessing whether I’d lost my work (even though that “saved” message could be more prominent). 
 - Account/security settings were easy to find and made sense. MFA sat where I’d expect (My Account), and the best part was having the option to receive verification by email, which I prefer because authenticator apps = “panic stations” when you change devices. 

Net: the portal flow is practical, it supports fast completion, gives you sensible controls, and doesn’t punish you for doing normal user behaviour like going back a step or re-uploading something."}

{"text": "Overall it was helpful and informative. Once I used the site search fuction (I am a big search function user), I could get to the right course quickly and the page was laid out in a way that made it easy to scan for what matters (apply/contact/next steps). \n\nWhat was missing for me was clearer visibility of Aboriginal student supports within the course journey (not just present somewhere on the site, but obvious and connected to applying/studying)."}

{"text": "Navigation in the enrolment portal was mostly easy and intuitive. The structure with tabs/sections made sense, and I could move through the key areas (profile, program search, form steps, uploads) without getting lost. \n\nWhere it became less intuitive was support pathways and a couple of \u201chidden\u201d elements: the feedback/reporting option wasn\u2019t obvious until I found the bottom-right widget, and the document upload area was confusing because files aren\u2019t actually visible to UQ unless they\u2019re attached to the application."}
{"selected": ["Screen reader"]}

{"text": "The most difficult / frustrating part was trying to work out how to report an issue or give feedback inside the portal — it felt needlessly hidden. 
 - I went where most people go first: I scrolled to the bottom and looked for Technical support / Contact us. I clicked Technical support and hit a dead end — it pushed me into something that assumed I already had the right type of account/access, which is exactly when you need support. 
 - Then I did what I always do: tried alternate links and even went “Control+F” hunting for anything that looked like “tech / technical” support for the digital team — still didn’t surface a clear “report a problem” pathway. 
 - Only after that did I discover the actual feedback entry point (the little widget / link sitting in the bottom-right corner). Once I found it, submitting feedback was easy — but finding it was the problem. 
 - The experience left me thinking there’s a “contact us is the absolute last resort” culture baked into the portal. That might work for general browsing, but when someone is mid-application (high stakes), you should make support/feedback far more visible and obvious. 

A close second frustration was the document upload logic. Drag-and-drop worked well, but then I hit a confusing message: “Attachments in my document list are not visible to the institution unless attached as part of the application.” That creates hesitation: why have a documents list tab at all if it doesn’t actually send anything through? It needs to be way louder and clearer (even visually — big warning text) so people don’t assume they’ve uploaded what the university can see."}

{"text": "1) The application form caused unnecessary friction (and trust issues) in key places

What happened:
 - On the Aboriginal/Torres Strait Islander question, there was no “Prefer not to say”, which is standard for sensitive self-ID. 
 - It also didn’t explain why you’re asking or what happens next (e.g., supports, opt-in contact), which made it feel like data collection rather than genuine support. 
 - I also felt the portal could be clearer about “save and come back” / what’s been saved — it was there, but not prominent enough to build confidence while completing the form. 

What I expected: For sensitive questions, I expect: choice, transparency, and control — including “prefer not to say,” a short explanation of purpose, and an option to be contacted by the relevant support unit (or not). 

Fix:
 - Add Prefer not to say. 
 - Add a one-liner: “We ask this to connect you to supports and monitor equity outcomes. Your response is optional.” 
 - Add an explicit opt-in: “Would you like to be contacted by the Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander student support team?” 
 - Make save status more visible throughout the form so people don’t feel like they’ll lose progress.

2) Reporting a problem / giving feedback was basically hidden

What happened: When I tried to report an issue, I did what most users do: went looking for Technical support and Contact us. Technical support took me to a dead end (it behaved like I didn’t have the right account/access), and I had to bounce around trying different paths. I didn’t naturally find the dedicated feedback option until it was pointed out (bottom-right). 

What I expected: If I’m logged in and applying, there should be an obvious “Report an issue / Get help” option that feels clearly tied to the portal team — not a scavenger hunt. 

Fix: Put a clear “Help / Report an issue” button in the main navigation or header (not just a corner widget), and make sure “Technical support” doesn’t route users into a dead end.

3) Document upload + “My documents” was confusing and easy to get wrong

What happened: Upload itself (drag/drop) was easy. But then I hit a confusing rule: documents in “My documents” aren’t visible to the institution unless attached to the application. That creates hesitation because you think you’ve uploaded something… but actually you haven’t submitted it in a way they can see. It made me question why the tab even exists if it doesn’t function like a real submission area.

What I expected: If there’s a “Documents” area, I expect it to behave like a proper document vault that’s clearly linked to submission — or at least tell me, loudly, what counts as “submitted.” 
Fix: Make the rule unmissable at the moment of upload (not buried as fine print). Example: a banner that says “Uploaded ≠ Submitted. Attach to your application to send to UQ.” and a direct “Attach to application” action from that screen."}
{"selected": ["More direct and helpful calls to action"]}

{"text": "1) Treat Indigenous identification like a trust moment, not a data grab. Improve the self-identification section by adding:
 - Prefer not to say
 - a plain-English “why we ask” statement
 - an opt-in: “Would you like Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander student support to contact you?” That turns it into a supportive, culturally safe experience.

2) Make “Help / Report an issue” impossible to miss. Right now, support/feedback feels hidden and I had to hunt for it. Put a persistent Help button in the header/menu (not a corner widget), with:
 - “Report a problem” (for portal bugs)
 - “Application help” (process/questions)
 - live status + expected response time
This is high-stakes admin — people shouldn’t be playing detective mid-application.

3) Build “requirements clarity” into the form (stop forcing users to leave). When I hit work experience / requirements, I wanted the portal to tell me exactly what UQ expects. Add:
 - a “What you need for this section” panel
 - direct link to the program’s entry requirements (in a new tab)
 - examples of acceptable evidence (CV, statement, employer letter, etc.)
This keeps people in flow and reduces guesswork.

4) Stronger progress reassurance. Make “Saved” and progress status more obvious (persistent banner or step tracker with autosave confirmation). People abandon forms when they don’t feel safe about losing work."}

{"text": "Before I even started searching, I had a handful of “deal-breaker” questions in my head:
 - Can I do this remotely / online, or am I realistically going to be forced on campus? (And if it’s blended, is block release a thing?) 
 - Who are the credible options across Australia? I’m looking for recognisable, reputable universities — the “big hitters” — not random providers I’ve never heard of. 
 - What’s the cost and value? Fees, fee schedules, and whether the program actually suits what I need — I’d compare providers properly once I’ve got options in front of me. 
 - Do I actually meet entry requirements? Do I need a previous degree, or can I get in based on work experience — and what evidence do they expect? 
 - What Aboriginal supports come with the university, and what supports come with self-identifying? I want to know what the support offer is, why they’re asking, and whether it’s genuine or I’m just going to feel like a number for reporting/funding. 
 - If I register an account, what happens to my data? If I hand over my email and phone number, is it protected or am I going to get spammed because it’s been shared/sold?"}
{"selected": "Understanding entry requirements"}

{"text": "I haven’t had disability-related accessibility barriers (I’m not using assistive tech), but I have had plenty of “accessibility” issues in the real-world sense, admin and digital systems that block you from doing simple study tasks. 

Here’s what’s bitten me before (and why it’s frustrating):
 - Getting locked out because of MFA/authenticator apps (device change = chaos).
I’ve been burnt badly by authenticator-based MFA when I’ve upgraded phones. You get a new device, then suddenly you can’t access what you need, and the recovery process is painful. I’m still locked out of historical emails and accounts because of this exact problem. That’s “panic stations” stuff — not because MFA is bad, but because the device-change pathway is often unclear and slow. 

Because of that, I often prefer email first, then SMS over authenticator apps, and I’ve even avoided MFA altogether at times because the lockout risk is worse than the security benefit for me. 
 - No clear “what to do if something goes wrong” support pathway.
When you’re studying, you don’t have time to play detective — you need a visible “help / report an issue” option that takes you to the right team. I’ve hit situations where “technical support” links are there but lead to dead ends or assume you’re already fully set up, and you waste time bouncing around contact pages. 
 - Control and certainty: I’m a heavy Ctrl+F and “show me the exact requirement” person.
If the system doesn’t clearly show requirements, status, and what’s been successfully submitted, it creates unnecessary stress because you’re second-guessing whether you’ve done the right thing."}

{"text": "The best study experience I’ve had is when the whole thing was designed like a system and not a maze.

It was great because everything that matters was obvious and predictable:
 - Clear structure from day one: weekly modules, what to read, what to do, and what “good” looks like (rubrics/examples). No guesswork.
 - Respectful, culturally safe feel: when Aboriginal supports were visible and real (not buried), it changed the tone. It felt like the institution expected Aboriginal people to be there, not like an afterthought and that feeling of network was incredible!
 - Online-first that actually worked: lectures/resources were easy to access, and I could do it around work and life without feeling punished for not being on campus.
 - Assessment expectations were crystal clear: entry/assessment requirements were spelled out in plain English, with templates or examples, so you weren’t guessing what evidence to provide.
 - Fast, human support when admin stuff broke: if there was a technical or enrolment issue, there was a visible help pathway and someone responded quickly — not “go search FAQs and good luck.”
 - One place to do everything: materials, submissions, grades, messages — not five logins and three different portals."}

{"text": "Compared to a lot of uni websites I’ve used, UQ’s public website is above average, it feels modern, clean, and “designed for scanning”, not reading a wall of text. The search worked properly (I found “Master of Business” fast, which is how I navigate) and the page design kept the next steps visible with those sticky pop-outs like Contact us / How to apply / Get ready, which is smart because it reduces wandering.

What it did better than most:
 - Not overloaded: it looked aesthetically pleasing and nothing felt “hidden”. 
 - Trust cues: diversity in imagery + reviews (that vulnerability actually helps credibility). 
 - Acknowledgement done better than token: the quick link from Acknowledgement of Country into reconciliation content felt innovative and more meaningful than the usual footer line.

Discoverability via Google wasn’t great when I searched for online business degrees, UQ wasn’t showing up early and the “big hitters” were. For remote/online students, that’s a real weakness."}
